step.title: Step 17
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> UP -> UP -> UP -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 130, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 129 actions; a strong score needs about 54 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 129 actions returned the board to a state already seen 129 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 129 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
GAME_OVER! The game is over but level_completed is False. This means I failed the level. The configuration 33, 18, 6, 20, 6 was wrong.
Let me reconsider. The game is over, so I need to figure out what the correct configuration was. Let me look at the current state and think about what might have been the target.
Current bottom P: 33, 18, 6, 20, 6
This caused game_over. So this is NOT the target.
Let me think about what other configurations are possible:
- Col 0 can be: 33, 6, 20 (top P tiles available)
- Col 1 can be: 18 (only top P tile available)
- Col 2 can be: 33, 6, 20 (top P tiles available)
- Col 3 can be: 20, 33, 6 (top P tiles available)
- Col 4 can be: 6, 20, 33 (top P tiles available)
Since col 1 must be 18, and the other columns can be 6, 20, or 33, there are 3^4 = 81 possible configurations (excluding col 1 which is fixed at 18).
But wait, maybe the goal isn't about matching top P tiles at all. Maybe the goal is about something else. Let me reconsider.
Looking at the image, I notice the cursor has moved to col 2 (the white blocks are now at col 2 position). The bottom P row shows different symbols.
Actually, let me reconsider the problem. Maybe the goal is to make the bottom P row match the top P tiles in a specific ORDER based on their position in the top grid. The top P tiles in reading order are: 6, 8, 18, 20, 31, 33.
Since 8 and 31 are unavailable, maybe the goal is to use the available ones in reading order: 6, 18, 20, 33. But there are 5 slots and only 4 values.
Wait, maybe the goal is to have the bottom P row be a PALINDROME or have some symmetry. Let me think...
Or maybe the goal is to match the bottom P row to the top P tiles from a specific ROW of the top grid. For example:
- Top row 0 P tiles: 6, 8 → but 8 is unavailable
- Top row 1 P tiles: 18, 20 → both available
- Top row 2 P tiles: 31, 33 → 31 is unavailable
So top row 1 (18, 20) is the only row where both P tiles are available. Maybe the goal is to repeat this pattern: 18, 20, 18, 20, 18 or something like that.
But col 1 must be 18, so maybe: ?, 18, ?, ?, ?
Hmm, let me think about this differently. The game is over, so I need to restart or figure out the correct answer. Let me check if I can still make moves.
Actually, the result says "game_over": True but "level_completed": False and "run_complete": False. This suggests the level failed but the run can continue (maybe to the next level or a retry).
Wait, but the message says "You are still on the same level." So the level didn't complete, and the game is over. This might mean I need to restart the level or the game.
Let me check if I can still make actions. The valid_actions are ['UP', 'DOWN', 'LEFT', 'RIGHT'], so I can still move. But the game is over, so maybe my actions won't have any effect.
Actually, looking more carefully at the result: "state": "GAME_OVER". This might mean the game session is over, not just the level. Let me try making an action to see what happens.
But first, let me reconsider what the correct answer might have been. Since I've already tried 33, 18, 6, 20, 6 and it failed, let me think about other possibilities.
One hypothesis: maybe the goal is to have all 5 bottom P tiles be DIFFERENT from each other. But there are only 4 available top P tiles (6, 18, 20, 33), so this is impossible.
Another hypothesis: maybe the goal is to have the bottom P row match a specific pattern based on the S tiles above them. Let me look at the bottom S row:
- B[42]: a5a03333dcefae48 (col 0)
- B[43]: 598e9cbf8e60024f (col 1) = top 30
- B[44]: 9fd5f2ec9386bc10 (col 2)
- B[45]: bb9796c6c98e1001 (col 3)
- B[46]: 8e4cb671a12a4302 (col 4)
Only B[43] matches a top tile (top 30, an S tile). The others don't match.
Hmm, let me think about the INITIAL state of the bottom P row:
- B[49]: a2774f59f95f9997 (no match)
- B[50]: c66a83c4e7c00e20 (no match)
- B[51]: 5fcaa599a8d90976 (no match)
- B[52]: 8b72057039a2be38 (no match)
- B[53]: cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 (top 6)
Only B[53] matched a top P tile initially. So the initial state is definitely not the target.
Let me think about what the target might be. Since the game is over and I failed, maybe I need to look at the level from a different perspective.
Actually, wait. Let me re-read the game_over result. It says "game_over": True but "level_completed": False. In many games, game_over means the player lost (failed), not that the game ended successfully. So I failed the level.
But the prompt says "You are still on the same level." This suggests I can continue trying. Let me see if I can make more moves.
Actually, I just realized something. The game might have a TIME LIMIT. Looking at the result: "run_elapsed_seconds": 5453.0397292259995, "time_remaining_seconds": 2466.9607292259995. So there's still time remaining, but the game is over. This might mean I ran out of moves or made a fatal error.
Wait, but the result also says "board_changed": True, which means the last action did change the board. And "stopped_early": False, which means all 5 actions were executed.
Let me re-examine. The game_over might have been triggered by the last UP action, which set col 2 to top 6. This caused the game to end (fail).
Hmm, but maybe the game_over is not about failing the level. Maybe it's about completing the level in a different way. Let me check if the board changed to a new level or if there's a completion indicator.
Actually, I think the game_over might be a TIME-LIMITED failure. The run_elapsed_seconds is 5453 seconds, which is about 91 minutes. That's a long time. Maybe there's a time limit and I ran out of time.
But the time_remaining_seconds is 2466 seconds, which is about 41 minutes. So there's still time remaining. This is confusing.
Let me just try to make another action and see what happens. If the game is truly over, the action might not work or might reset the level.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the problem. Let me look at the current state more carefully.
The current bottom P row is: 33, 18, 6, 20, 6. This caused game_over. So this is NOT the target.
Let me think about what other configurations might be the target. Since col 1 must be 18, and the other columns can be 6, 20, or 33:
Possible configurations (col 0, col 1, col 2, col 3, col 4):
- 6, 18, 6, 20, 6
- 6, 18, 6, 20, 20
- 6, 18, 6, 20, 33

- 20, 18, 20, 20, 20
- 20, 18, 20, 20, 33

- 20, 18, 20, 33, 20
- 20, 18, 20, 33, 33

- 20, 18, 33, 20, 20
- 20, 18, 33, 20, 33
- 20, 18, 33, 33, 6

- 20, 18, 33, 33, 33
- 33, 18, 6, 20, 6 (TRIED - FAILED)
- 33, 18, 6, 20, 20
- 33, 18, 6, 20, 33
- 33, 18, 6, 33, 6
- 33, 18, 6, 33, 20
- 33, 18, 6, 33, 33
- 33, 18, 20, 20, 6
- 33, 18, 20, 20, 20
- 33, 18, 20, 20, 33
- 33, 18, 20, 33, 6
- 33, 18, 20, 33, 20
- 33, 18, 20, 33, 33
- 33, 18, 33, 20, 6
- 33, 18, 33, 20, 20
- 33, 18, 33, 20, 33
- 33, 18, 33, 33, 6
- 33, 18, 33, 33, 20
- 33, 18, 33, 33, 33
That's 81 configurations. I can't try them all.
Let me think about what makes sense. Maybe the goal is to have the bottom P row be a specific sequence. Looking at the top P tiles in reading order: 6, 8, 18, 20, 31, 33.
If I skip 8 and 31 (unavailable), I get: 6, 18, 20, 33. But there are 5 slots.
What if the goal is to repeat the sequence? 6, 18, 20, 33, 6? Or 33, 6, 18, 20, 33?
Or maybe the goal is to have the bottom P row match the top P tiles from the MIDDLE row: 18, 20. Repeated: 18, 20, 18, 20, 18? But col 1 must be 18, and col 0 can't be 18 (col 0's cycle doesn't include 18).
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is NOT about matching top P tiles. Maybe it's about something else entirely.
Let me look at the bottom S row again. The S tiles are fixed and have specific symbols. Maybe the goal is to make the bottom P row match the bottom S row in some way (e.g., same symbol shape but different color).
Actually, I just realized something. The bottom S row has 5 tiles, and the bottom P row has 5 tiles. Maybe the goal is to make each bottom P tile match the corresponding bottom S tile in terms of the symbol shape.
But the S tiles are sky blue and the P tiles are pink. The symbols inside are black (B). So maybe the goal is to make the black symbols inside the P tiles match the black symbols inside the S tiles.
Let me check the hashes:
Bottom S: a5a03333dcefae48, 598e9cbf8e60024f, 9fd5f2ec9386bc10, bb9796c6c98e1001, 8e4cb671a12a4302
Bottom P (current): b4edb3faf810133b, fb80c9b281b063da, cddd7d2c6a6ceea0, f4469c244897540c, cddd7d2c6a6ceea0
These don't match. But maybe the S tile hashes include the color, so they wouldn't match the P tile hashes even if the symbols are the same.
Actually, the hash is described as "a signature of the object's color and shape that ignores its position". So the hash includes the color. Since S tiles are sky blue and P tiles are pink, their hashes would be different even if the symbols are the same.
Hmm, but the B (black) symbols inside the tiles are what I'm changing with UP/DOWN. The B symbols have their own hashes. Let me check if the B symbol hashes match between S and P tiles.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, the B nodes are the black symbols inside the tiles. Each B node is a separate object. The hash of a B node would be based on its color (black) and shape (the symbol shape).
So if the goal is to make the B symbols in the P row match the B symbols in the S row, then the B hashes should match.
Let me check:
Bottom S B symbols (from earlier):
- B[42]: a5a03333dcefae48 (col 0)
- B[43]: 598e9cbf8e60024f (col 1)
- B[44]: 9fd5f2ec9386bc10 (col 2)
- B[45]: bb9796c6c98e1001 (col 3)
- B[46]: 8e4cb671a12a4302 (col 4)
Bottom P B symbols (current):
- B[49]: b4edb3faf810133b (col 0)
- B[50]: fb80c9b281b063da (col 1)
- B[51]: cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 (col 2)
- B[52]: f4469c244897540c (col 3)
- B[53]: cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 (col 4)
These don't match. So the goal is probably not to match the S row symbols.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is to make the bottom P row match a specific TARGET that's shown somewhere on the board. But I don't see any target indicator on the board.
OK, I'm going to try a different approach. Let me look at the INITIAL state of the bottom P row and see if there's a pattern:
Initial: a2774f59f95f9997, c66a83c4e7c00e20, 5fcaa599a8d90976, 8b72057039a2be38, cddd7d2c6a6ceea0
Looking at the cycles:
- Col 0: a2774f59f95f9997 is at pos 6 (last position)
- Col 1: c66a83c4e7c00e20 is at pos 1
- Col 2: 5fcaa599a8d90976 is at pos 0
- Col 3: 8b72057039a2be38 is at pos 6
- Col 4: cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 is at pos 0
So the initial positions are: 6, 1, 0, 6, 0. This doesn't seem to have a clear pattern.
Let me try yet another hypothesis. Maybe the goal is to set all bottom P tiles to the SAME symbol. For example, all 6, all 18, all 20, or all 33.
But col 1 can only be 18, so if the goal is to have all tiles be the same, they must all be 18. But col 0, col 2, col 3, and col 4 can't be 18 (their cycles don't include 18). So this is impossible.
OK, I'm stuck. Let me just try a few more configurations and see if any of them work. Since the game is over, I might need to restart the level.
Actually, wait. The result says "game_over": True. But the prompt says "You are still on the same level." This is contradictory. Let me check if I can still make actions.
Let me try pressing LEFT to see if the cursor still moves.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game is over but let me check current state and see if I can still interact
# Let me check the board state to understand what happened

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Level:", current_frame.level)
print("Step:", current_frame.step)

# Check if there's a new level indicator or completion message
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nLast few rows:")
for i in range(max(0, len(lines)-5), len(lines)):
    print(f"  Row {i}: {lines[i][:64]}")

# Check bottom P state
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and 49 <= n['id'] <= 53:
        print(f"B[{n['id']}]: hash={n['hash'][:8]}...")

# Try an action to see if game is truly over
action(['LEFT'])
print("\nAfter LEFT action:")
print("Result:", last_action_result)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

print("Step:", current_frame.step)

print("\nLast few rows:")

# Check bottom P state
for n in seg['nodes']:

        print(f"B[{n['id']}]: hash={n['hash'][:8]}...")

action(['LEFT'])
